items: Inversion performance remains limited due to coil architecture and element pairing (16Tx driven by 8 RFPA channels), constraining whole-brain uniform T1-weighted imaging.
Only second-order B0 shimming; unclear how higher-order shims or dynamic shimming would further reduce off-resonance and benefit EPI/fMRI.
Inter-field comparisons (3T vs 7T vs 11.7T) use different subjects and do not include quantitative SNR/CNR, tSNR, or g-factor analyses; conclusions on SNR scaling are primarily qualitative.
Safety results are reported as “no significant differences” without effect sizes, confidence intervals, or power/equivalence analyses; potential for Type II error is not addressed.
Lack of quantitative image quality metrics (e.g., flip-angle NRMSE maps per subject, spatially resolved SNR/CNR, laminar contrast line profiles) to substantiate “mesoscale” claims.
Key safety and performance metrics are relegated to “extended data”; the main text would benefit from summarizing statistics (e.g., p-values/CI for vestibular/cognitive, micronuclei rate changes).
Limited detail on SAR margins (peak local/global SAR vs IEC limits, margins consumed during each sequence, thermal modeling assumptions, and monitoring).
Context with prior ultra-high-field human imaging at 9.4T and 10.5T (beyond safety) could be expanded to better position imaging performance and RF strategies.

Limited discussion of alternative non-pTx strategies (e.g., dielectric shimming, multi-spoke universal pulses vs subject-specific pulses, or sequence designs with built-in B1+/Δf0 correction) and their trade-offs at 11.7T.
The fit quality (reduced χ² ≈ 2.0) remains above unity even after including the 3S component, suggesting either underestimation of uncertainties or incomplete modeling of lineshape/systematics.
Heavy reliance on simulation-derived power corrections C(f) and field maps without an experimental cross-check of microwave field amplitudes/phases and their frequency dependence.
Two-level approximation under linearly polarized fields may not fully capture multi-level couplings and residual Zeeman structure in weak stray fields.
Sparse frequency sampling (seven points plus “TL off”) limits robustness of lineshape determination and may increase sensitivity to modeling assumptions and background subtraction.

The 3S “observation” is inferred from fit improvement and amplitude ratios, lacking an independent control knob (e.g., adjustable E-field quench upstream) to modulate only the n = 3 population for verification.
No demonstrated power-scan (Rabi/AC-Stark scaling) to validate the simulation-based power corrections and to bound residual AC-Stark or saturation effects empirically.
Some notational inconsistencies (e.g., mF vs MF) and scattered details on field calibration and background stability make it hard to fully reproduce the systematic estimates.
The correlation between parameters in the composite fit (SBKG, B2S, B3S, foffset) is not reported, hindering assessment of parameter degeneracies.

A more direct discussion of alternative high-precision schemes (e.g., separated-oscillatory-fields/Ramsey for narrowing) and how this measurement informs or transitions to those techniques would strengthen the outlook.
Limited quantitative comparison to prior muonium LS measurements on lineshape systematics (e.g., line pulling from neighboring hyperfine components) beyond statistical improvements.

The chosen relativistic two-body P-wave Breit–Wigner model is an approximate description for a three-body D0D0π+ final state near an S-wave D*D threshold with finite D* width; threshold dynamics and coupled-channel effects are likely important.

Reliance on Wilks’ theorem for significance estimates near kinematic thresholds and under parameter boundaries may not be fully reliable, though the very large observed significance likely renders this a minor issue for discovery claims.

Use of sPlot-based statistical subtraction assumes negligible residual correlations between the discriminating D0 masses and the D0D0π+ mass; while cross-checked with tight mass windows, a quantitative assessment of residual bias would strengthen the claim.

The analysis largely focuses on establishing the signal and determining δmBW, ΓBW, without presenting kinematic differentials (pT, rapidity) or production rates, which could offer early discrimination between molecular and compact scenarios.
Minor notation inconsistencies (e.g., occasional Tcc++ vs Tcc+) and typographical artifacts from PDF extraction; while not detrimental, a thorough pass for consistency would improve readability.
The motivation for the P-wave “two-body” AB construction (with mA = 2mD0 and mB = mπ) would benefit from a clearer justification and discussion of its limitations in the near-threshold regime.

The Letter could more explicitly situate its findings relative to lattice-QCD results on I = 1 DD(*) scattering (no near-threshold poles) and the emerging consensus that Tcc is an I = 0 (1+) state, emphasizing complementarity.
Brief mention of expectations from effective-field-theory and Flatté-like parameterizations near D*D thresholds would better contextualize the chosen lineshape and its limitations.

Assumption of identical mass-shape PDFs for baryon and antibaryon components in simultaneous fits could bias yield asymmetries if small charge-dependent resolution or tail effects persist; while alternatives were tried, explicit asymmetry in shape parameters was not explored.

The reliance on fixed signal-tail parameters from simulation (for some components) can be a source of bias; a more extensive floating of shape parameters or per-subsample calibration would further de-risk the extraction.

The nuisance-asymmetry cancellation hinges on accurate kinematic matching between signal and control; while addressed, residual mismodelling across the four localized phase-space regions could differ from the global case.
The BDT training description does not explicitly demonstrate charge-symmetry of inputs/response; absent that, a small charge-dependent selection efficiency could leak into the raw asymmetry.
Limited discussion of potential Λ0_b polarization differences between baryon and antibaryon (expected small at LHCb, but could interplay with angular acceptance in a four-body final state).

For localized ACP results, it is not fully detailed whether all nuisance-asymmetry corrections and their uncertainties were recomputed in each region (with region-specific kinematics) and how region-to-region correlations were propagated.

Tabulated breakdowns per subsample (year, polarity, trigger category) of raw and corrected asymmetries are referenced but not shown; including them would strengthen transparency and aid external scrutiny.

The selected phase-space regions are motivated by resonance content, but the predefinition procedure and protection against selection-induced trials (beyond the look-elsewhere correction) could be described more explicitly.

The paper situates itself well relative to earlier null results in charmless Λ0_b decays, but could add a brief contrast with the recent evidence for CPV in Λ0_b → J/ψ pπ− vs J/ψ pK− (ΔACP ≈ 4%, ~3.9σ when combined), to contextualize the emerging picture of CPV in baryons across different topologies.

The amplitude relies on isospin symmetry for Tcc+ → D*D couplings; isospin breaking beyond mass/width differences may be non-negligible at the O(100 keV) level near threshold and could bias extracted scattering parameters.

The fit fixes |g| to a large value in the baseline, placing the model in a scaling regime; although lower limits are quoted, some physical quantities (effective range r, compositeness Z) become weakly constrained and model-dependent.
Wilks’ theorem is used for significances and |g| profiling in a regime with parameters near physical boundaries and non-Gaussian likelihoods; this can be optimistic without toy-MC validation.

The background model is largely parametric (phase-space × polynomial). While variants are explored, a purely data-driven cross-check (e.g., mass-sideband or wrong-sign control regions) near threshold would further de-risk shape biases that could mimic cusp-like enhancements.

The abstract mentions an “unexpected dependence of the production rate on track multiplicity,” but no quantitative results or systematics for production observables are provided here (at least in the provided text).
Relative-yield comparisons among D0D0π+, D0D0, D+D0 require careful efficiency corrections across different kinematic/topological selections; details are referenced to Methods but not visible here.

Several key details reside in Methods (e.g., explicit amplitude equations, treatment of second-sheet continuation, mapping to A_NR for scattering parameters), making it difficult to fully assess technical assumptions from the main text alone.
The compositeness Z limit is discussed qualitatively; a numerical upper limit and its confidence level would improve clarity.

While the paper cites relevant phenomenology and Tcc+ theory broadly, explicit comparison with modern EFT-based treatments of near-threshold charm-meson molecules (e.g., XEFT-inspired) would sharpen the connection between extracted parameters and theoretical expectations.

A higher-level positioning relative to the broader exotic-hadrons literature (as summarized in 2207.14594) could be expanded, especially regarding how the scattering parameters compare across molecular candidates (e.g., X(3872)).
